title: 1 Chronicles 10
subtitle: The Death of Saul — and Why

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Saul Anointed King

sections.header: Verses 1–7 — The Battle of Gilboa; Saul and His Sons Die

refs.note: The parallel account in Samuel, which the Chronicler follows closely but condenses. Samuel provides the broader context; Chronicles provides the theological verdict.

notes.note: Saul’s death on the battlefield is the culmination of a reign defined by disobedience. The Chronicler lets the death speak for itself before adding the theological commentary. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The phrase “all his house” (kol-bēytō) is a Chronicler’s addition not found in 1 Sam 31:6. It serves a literary rather than historical function: closing the Saulide chapter definitively. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: The narrative begins abruptly: “Now the Philistines fought against Israel.” No backstory, no Saul narratives, no anointing, no David-and-Goliath — the Chronicler drops the reader directly into the final battle. Saul and three sons (including Jonathan) die on Mount Gilboa. The Israelites flee and the Philistines occupy their towns. The Chronicler compresses 1 Samuel 31 into seven verses, keeping only what serves his purpose: the death that makes room for David.

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew mālaḵ (to reign) carries the weight of divinely delegated authority. In the Chronicler's theology, every king reigns as God's representative — their faithfulness or failure directly affects the nation's destiny. The verb appears at the critical transitions that shape Israel's monarchic history.

notes.note: The military conflicts recorded here illustrate a consistent biblical principle: victory belongs not to the largest army but to those who trust in the Lord (2 Chr 20:15). When Israel relied on foreign alliances rather than God, defeat followed. When they trusted God alone, even overwhelming odds were overcome. Believers face the same choice in every spiritual battle (Eph 6:10-18).

The outcome of every military engagement in biblical history ultimately reflects the spiritual condition of God's people. When the king and nation walked in faithfulness, God fought for them. When they trusted in human alliances or military strength, they were humbled. The application for believers is clear: our warfare is spiritual, and victory depends on faith, not flesh (2 Cor 10:3-5).

The spiritual application for believers is that our battle is not against flesh and blood but against spiritual forces of evil (Eph 6:12). The same God who granted victory to faithful kings fights for His people today. Our responsibility is to trust and obey; the outcome belongs to the Lord. 'Thanks be to God, who gives us the victory through our Lord Jesus Christ' (1 Cor 15:57).

sections.header: Verses 8–14 — Philistine Desecration; the Chronicler’s Verdict

heb.paragraph: Saul died “because of his unfaithfulness” (maʿal, v.13) — the same word used for the Transjordanian tribes’ exile (5:25). The Chronicler uses one word to explain both individual failure and national catastrophe. Unfaithfulness is the universal cause of disaster.

refs.note: Saul consults the medium at Endor — the specific act the Chronicler cites as a cause of death.
Samuel’s verdict on Saul: “Because you have rejected the word of the LORD, he has rejected you as king.” The Chronicler condenses this into “not keeping the LORD’s word.”

notes.note: The statement that “the LORD put him to death” teaches that even death in battle is under God’s sovereignty. What appears to be military defeat is in fact divine judgment. Nothing happens outside God’s government. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: lishʾōl bĕʾōb (“to inquire of a medium”) — the verb shāʾal (“to ask, inquire”) is the same root used for “seeking” God. Saul sought, but from the wrong source. The Chronicler’s theology hinges on the direction of one’s seeking. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: The Philistines find the bodies, strip Saul’s armour, and put his head in the temple of Dagon. But the men of Jabesh Gilead rescue the bodies (their gratitude for Saul’s early victory, 1 Sam 11). Then the Chronicler delivers his theological verdict — absent from Samuel: Saul died because of (1) unfaithfulness to the LORD, (2) not keeping the LORD’s word, and (3) consulting a medium. “So the LORD put him to death and turned the kingdom over to David son of Jesse.” Three causes, one consequence, one successor.

notes.note: The Chronicler records this history not merely as political narrative but as theological instruction. Every event demonstrates God's sovereign governance of His people and His unwavering faithfulness to the Davidic covenant. The believing reader is meant to see in these events the pattern of God's dealings with His church in every age.

The Chronicler's perspective consistently emphasizes God's faithfulness to His covenant promises despite Israel's unfaithfulness. This dual theme — human failure and divine grace — is the heartbeat of all Scripture and finds its ultimate resolution in the cross of Christ, where God's justice and mercy meet perfectly (Rom 3:25-26).

The Chronicler wrote for a post-exilic community needing to understand their identity and hope. His message remains vital: God's promises to David are irrevocable, worship is the center of covenant life, and faithfulness — not circumstances — determines blessing. Believers today can take the same comfort: our God is faithful even when we are not (2 Tim 2:13).

ppl.text: Nathan is not mentioned in 1 Chronicles 10. The key figures are Saul, his sons, and David.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVThe Philistines were in hot pursuit of Saul and his sons, and they killed his sons Jonathan, Abinadab and Malki-Shua.ESVAnd the Philistines overtook Saul and his sons, and the Philistines struck down Jonathan and Abinadab and Malchi-shua, the sons of Saul.NIVWhen all the Israelites in the valley saw that the army had fled and that Saul and his sons had died, they abandoned their towns and fled. And the Philistines came and occupied them.ESVAnd when all the men of Israel who were in the valley saw that the army had fled and that Saul and his sons were dead, they abandoned their cities and fled, and the Philistines came and lived in them.

themes.note: The Death of Saul — and Why

coaching.tip: The Chronicler covers Saul's entire reign in one chapter — just his death and its theological explanation: 'Saul died because he was unfaithful to the LORD.' No Goliath, no Jonathan, no David's fugitive years. Everything before David is prelude; the real story starts now.

prayer_prompt: Saul died because he was unfaithful and consulted a medium instead of the Lord. Consider asking God about the substitutes for His guidance you've been tempted to consult.
